As part of a growing anti-tech movement, startup dumb.co is pushing flip phones as a way for young people to find ‘social and spiritual freedom’
“They aren’t as dumb as they look,” our facilitator said, referring to the dark gray flip phone in his hand. He just as easily could ha
articles.titleZh: 太愚蠢了，它可能会起作用：这些愚蠢的手机传播者能说服你放弃智能手机吗？
articles.summaryZh:
作为不断发展的反科技运动的一部分，初创公司 dumb.co 正在推广翻盖手机，以此作为年轻人寻找“社交和精神自由”的一种方式
“他们并不像看上去那么愚蠢，”我们的协调员指着他手中的深灰色翻盖手机说道。他同样可以轻松地

contentParagraphs.en: “They aren’t as dumb as they look,” our facilitator said, referring to the dark gray flip phone in his hand. He just as easily could have been talking about us, the 28 New York residents before him who had signed up to use the device for the entire month of March. He explained that the relic was loaded with WhatsApp, iMessage, Google Maps, Uber, Microsoft 2FA – nothing like my seventh-grade flip phone.We each had paid $75 to participate in Month Offline, or MO, a program that challenged us to swear off our smartphones entirely. Another $25 went to dumb.co, the company behind MO, for the so-called dumbphones we would use as we navigated daily life.In its early days, MO had given out flip phones so dumb – no maps, no ride-sharing, iffy texting – that participants struggled to use them during and after the month-long experiment. So my MO cohort was the first to test dumb.co’s souped-up version, a discontinued TCL Flip 2 bolstered with the messaging and mapping functions many of us are dependent upon.A retrofitted TCL Flip 2 at the dumb.co launch party dinner in New York City on 1 May. Photograph: Jacques Morel/Courtesy of dumb.coI was skeptical. Most of us came to MO sick of being glued to the supercomputers in our palms, angling hours of our attention downwards each day. Disaffected with tech companies and their devices, we were eager to dabble in the growing analog movement and compelled by MO’s mission to restore “our social, psychological, and spiritual freedom”, as per the offline pledge we signed on day one.But if “less is MO,” why was the dumbphone being enabled to do more?Was dumb.co authentic in its offline aspirations, or was it really a tech startup trying to ride the anti-tech zeitgeist?“We all have iPhones,” Danny Hogenkamp, CEO and co-founder of dumb.co, told me of his nine-person team. Their headquarters in Washington DC was soon to be flush with summer interns. “They just don’t leave the office.”Hogenkamp, 31, founded his first company, a DC data and technology firm called Grassroots Analytics specializing in fundraising for progressive causes, when he was just 22 years old. It became ground zero for his “dumbing down”. After instituting device-free meetings when his team returned to the office after Covid-19 lockdowns, Hogenkamp observed an increase in creativity and productivity – from his staff and himself.Hogenkamp’s experimentation with de-digitizing escalated to the point when, in 2023, his roommate gave him a flip phone as a joke. The phone, Hognekamp said, “barely worked” – which is to say, it really worked. In addition to gaining back what would have otherwise been screen time, Hogenkamp noticed he simply felt better. (Indeed, research papers have indicated that low levels of screen time are linked to better quality of life in children and adults.)Danny Hogenkamp, dumb.co’s CEO, at the New York event. Photograph: Jacques Morel/Courtesy of dumb.coAnd so Hogenkamp decided that the future is dumb. With the money he made from his first company, he tried seven different “dumb ideas”, including investing in an app that let users bet against each other’s screen time, a phone-free bar in DC, and various charity and political efforts. He also bought 150 Yondr pouches, fabric bags that phones are magnetically locked into, and started hosting his own phone-free parties in DC just for fun. “I was the pouch guy,” he said.In 2024, Hogenkamp and his pouches were summoned to enable a phone-free Shabbat dinner. Grant Besner, 29, who had recently moved to DC after stints on a regenerative farm in Virginia and as a natural building apprentice in upstate New York, showed up too. When Hogenkamp tried to check his phone in at the door, Besner held up a low-tech phone of his own – a habit he picked up after struggling to write his master’s thesis with his smartphone lurking nearby.Hogenkamp and Besner were both gobsmacked; it was rare to meet a fellow traveler. They became friends and started brainstorming, eventually coming up with the idea for MO in early 2025. “We’re probably going into this dystopian hellscape, where everyone is a slave to their device,” Besner said of their thought process. “But what if there was a different future where we branched off and everyone started getting flip phones? Might as well try that.”Hogenkamp, who was funding the project, bought flip phones for MO participants to borrow and enrolled in the first two cohorts in DC. While happily participating (and realizing his “lifelong dream to hang out with other people with flip phones”), Hogenkamp noticed that people were having “deeply spiritual experiences”, with many wanting to keep their flip phones at the end of the month. “How do we make that happen for them? I guess that’s just what a phone company is,” he said.Before starting dumb.co, Hogenkamp hosted phone-free parties in DC using Yondr pouches, fabric bags that phones are magnetically locked into. Photograph: Jacques Morel/Courtesy of dumb.coThis sense of shared awakening underpins many of the other neo-luddite communities sprouting up offline, with analog advocacy groups such as the Luddite Club hosting regular chapter meetings across the country, and the New York-based Strother School of Radical Attention facilitating workshops to “build sanctuaries of attention” and protest “coercive digital technologies”. MO participants in my own cohort shared that “flipping off” their smartphones felt like waking up. We got lost, talked to strangers, and had the disturbing and unending experience of being the only person looking up in a subway car. A sense of enlightenment seemed to emerge simply from being more mindful (a conclusion I imagine will prompt Buddhists to think: well, duh).By summer 2025, participants were given the option to keep their TCL Flip 2s after MO ended. In December, Hogenkamp retired from Grassroots to focus on dumb.co – a tech startup, lifestyle brand, and yes, phone company – building a team of loosely anti-tech gen Zers, many plucked
contentParagraphs.zh: “他们并不像看上去那么愚蠢，”我们的协调员指着他手中的深灰色翻盖手机说道。他也可以轻松地谈论我们，即在他之前签约使用该设备整个 3 月的 28 名纽约居民。他解释说，这个遗物上装满了 WhatsApp、iMessage、谷歌地图、Uber、微软 2FA——完全不像我七年级的翻盖手机。我们每人支付了 75 美元参加了“离线月”（MO）计划，该计划要求我们完全放弃智能手机。另外 25 美元捐给了 mud.co，MO 背后的公司，用于购买我们在日常生活中使用的所谓哑手机。在早期，MO 发放的翻盖手机非常笨——没有地图，没有拼车，发短信也不稳定——以至于参与者在长达一个月的实验期间和之后都很难使用它们。因此，我的 MO 团队是第一个测试 dumb.co 增强版的人，这是一款已停产的 TCL Flip 2，增强了我们许多人所依赖的消息传递和地图功能。 5 月 1 日，在纽约举办的 dumb.co 发布会晚宴上，改装了 TCL Flip 2。照片：Jacques Morel/dumb.co 提供我对此表示怀疑。我们中的大多数人来到密苏里州后，都厌倦了被手中的超级计算机所束缚，每天都要花几个小时的注意力。由于对科技公司及其设备感到不满，我们渴望涉足日益增长的模拟运动，并被 MO 的使命所迫，按照我们第一天签署的线下承诺，恢复“我们的社会、心理和精神自由”。但如果“MO 越少”，为什么哑手机能够做得更多呢？dumb.co 的线下愿望是真实的，还是真的是一家试图顺应反科技时代精神的科技初创公司？“我们都有 iPhone，” dumb.co 的首席执行官兼联合创始人丹尼·霍根坎普 (Danny Hogenkamp) 向我讲述了他的九人团队。他们位于华盛顿特区的总部很快就挤满了暑期实习生。 “他们就是不离开办公室。”31 岁的霍根坎普创立了他的第一家公司，这是一家名为 Grassroots Analytics 的华盛顿数据和技术公司，专门为进步事业筹款，当时他只有 22 岁。他的“愚钝”变成了归零。在 Covid-19 封锁后，当他的团队返回办公室时，霍根坎普开始了无设备会议，他发现员工和他自己的创造力和生产力都得到了提高。霍根坎普的去数字化实验升级到了2023年，他的室友开玩笑地给了他一部翻盖手机。霍格内坎普说，这款手机“几乎不能用”——也就是说，它确实能用。霍根坎普发现，除了重新获得屏幕时间之外，他还感觉好多了。 （事实上​​，研究论文表明，较少的屏幕时间与儿童和成人更好的生活质量有关。）dumb.co 首席执行官丹尼·霍根坎普 (Danny Hogenkamp) 在纽约活动上。摄影：Jacques Morel/dumb.co 提供因此 Hogenkamp 认为未来是愚蠢的。用他从第一家公司赚到的钱，他尝试了七种不同的“愚蠢的想法”，包括投资一款让用户可以相互打赌屏幕时间的应用程序、在华盛顿特区开设一个没有电话的酒吧，以及各种慈善和政治努力。他还购买了 150 个 Yondr 袋子，即用磁力将手机锁在里面的布袋，并开始在华盛顿举办自己的无手机派对，只是为了好玩。他说：“我是袋子里的人。”2024 年，霍根坎普和他的袋子被召集起来，以实现无电话的安息日晚餐。 29 岁的格兰特·贝斯纳 (Grant Besner) 也出现了，他在弗吉尼亚州的一个再生农场工作了一段时间，并在纽约州北部做过自然建筑学徒，最近搬到了华盛顿。当霍根坎普试图在门口检查他的手机时，贝斯纳举起了自己的一部低科技手机——这是他在智能手机潜藏在附近的情况下努力写硕士论文后养成的习惯。霍根坎普和贝斯纳都目瞪口呆；难得遇见同路人。他们成为了朋友，开始集思广益，最终在 2025 年初提出了 MO 的想法。“我们可能会进入这个反乌托邦的地狱，每个人都是他们设备的奴隶，”贝斯纳谈到他们的思考过程时说道。 “但是，如果有一个不同的未来，我们分门别类，每个人都开始使用翻盖手机呢？不妨尝试一下。”资助该项目的霍根坎普购买了翻盖手机供 MO 参与者借用，并参加了华盛顿特区的前两个队列。霍根坎普在愉快地参与其中（并实现了他“用翻盖手机与其他人一起出去玩的毕生梦想”）的同时，注意到人们正在经历“深刻的精神体验”，许多人希望在月底保留他们的翻盖手机。他说：“我们如何才能让他们实现这一目标？我想这就是电话公司。”在创办 dumb.co 之前，Hogenkamp 使用 Yondr 袋（手机被磁性锁定在其中的布袋）在华盛顿举办了无电话派对。照片：Jacques Morel/Courtesy of mud.co 这种共同的觉醒感支撑着许多其他新勒德分子社区在线下兴起，类似的倡导团体如勒德分子俱乐部在全国各地定期举办分会会议，总部位于纽约的斯特罗瑟激进注意力学院举办研讨会，以“建立注意力避难所”并抗议“强制性数字技术”。我自己的队列中的 MO 参与者分享说，“关闭”他们的智能手机感觉就像醒来一样。我们迷路了，和陌生人交谈，并经历了成为地铁车厢里唯一抬头看的人的令人不安和无休止的经历。一种启蒙感似乎只是从更加正念中出现（我想这个结论会促使佛教徒思考：好吧，废话）。到 2025 年夏天，参与者可以选择在 MO 结束后保留​​他们的 TCL Flip 2。去年 12 月，Hogenkamp 从 Grassroots 退休，专注于 dumb.co——一家科技初创公司、生活方式品牌，当然还有电话公司——建立了一支由松散的反科技 Z 世代组成的团队，其中许多人都是被挑选出来的

articles.title: Opportunities shrinking for too many young people, says major report on 'lost generation'
articles.summary: A report warns the number of 16 to 24-year-olds out of work, education or training is set to rise to 1.25 million by 2031.
articles.titleZh: 关于“迷失的一代”的重要报告称太多年轻人的机会正在减少
articles.summaryZh: 一份报告警告称，到 2031 年，失业、教育或培训的 16 至 24 岁青少年人数将增至 125 万人。

articles.title: Happy, who taught researchers that elephants can recognize themselves, is euthanized
articles.summary: Happy, a Bronx Zoo elephant who gave researchers new insight into the animal's behavior and became the crux of a closely watched animal rights case, has been euthanized at age 55.
articles.titleZh: 哈皮（Happy）曾教导研究人员大象可以认出自己，却被安乐死
articles.summaryZh: 布朗克斯动物园的大象哈皮 (Happy) 在 55 岁时被安乐死，它让研究人员对动物的行为有了新的认识，并成为备受关注的动物权利案件的关键。

contentParagraphs.en: Bronx Zoo elephant "Happy" strolls inside the zoo's Asia Habitat in New York, Oct. 2, 2018.
contentParagraphs.zh: Bronx Zoo elephant "Happy" strolls inside the zoo's Asia Habitat in New York, Oct. 2, 2018.

contentParagraphs.en: NEW YORK — Happy, a Bronx Zoo elephant who gave researchers new insight into the animal's behavior and became the crux of a closely watched animal rights case, has been euthanized at age 55, the zoo said Wednesday.
contentParagraphs.zh: NEW YORK — Happy, a Bronx Zoo elephant who gave researchers new insight into the animal's behavior and became the crux of a closely watched animal rights case, has been euthanized at age 55, the zoo said Wednesday.

contentParagraphs.en: The Asian elephant was put to sleep Tuesday at the zoo where she lived for almost a half-century. Zoo officials said some age-related conditions accelerated in recent weeks, and she showed signs of a falloff in kidney or liver function. A necropsy revealed arthritis and large, inoperable uterine tumors that are impossible to diagnose in elephants through exams or imaging, the zoo said.
contentParagraphs.zh: The Asian elephant was put to sleep Tuesday at the zoo where she lived for almost a half-century. Zoo officials said some age-related conditions accelerated in recent weeks, and she showed signs of a falloff in kidney or liver function. A necropsy revealed arthritis and large, inoperable uterine tumors that are impossible to diagnose in elephants through exams or imaging, the zoo said.

contentParagraphs.en: "She was a wonderful elephant," interim zoo director Craig Piper said in an interview Wednesday, as heavy-hearted staffers absorbed the loss of an animal some had tended for over 30 years. "She served as a tremendous ambassador for elephants and for elephant conservation."
contentParagraphs.zh: "She was a wonderful elephant," interim zoo director Craig Piper said in an interview Wednesday, as heavy-hearted staffers absorbed the loss of an animal some had tended for over 30 years. "She served as a tremendous ambassador for elephants and for elephant conservation."

contentParagraphs.en: Since Happy's death, the zoo's 57-year-old Patty is the last elephant on exhibit in the United States' largest city. The zoo's parent institution, the Wildlife Conservation Society, decided 20 years ago to stop acquiring pachyderms.
contentParagraphs.zh: Since Happy's death, the zoo's 57-year-old Patty is the last elephant on exhibit in the United States' largest city. The zoo's parent institution, the Wildlife Conservation Society, decided 20 years ago to stop acquiring pachyderms.

contentParagraphs.en: Born in the wild in Asia, Happy was brought to the U.S. as a 1-year-old. She was named for a character from "Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs" before arriving at the zoo in 1977.
contentParagraphs.zh: Born in the wild in Asia, Happy was brought to the U.S. as a 1-year-old. She was named for a character from "Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs" before arriving at the zoo in 1977.

contentParagraphs.en: Happy keenly engaged with her keepers and was easy to motivate with favorite treats, such as watermelon or strawberries, said Keith Lovett, the zoo's director of animal programs. Piper said she sometimes stashed treats in her ear to save for later.
contentParagraphs.zh: Happy keenly engaged with her keepers and was easy to motivate with favorite treats, such as watermelon or strawberries, said Keith Lovett, the zoo's director of animal programs. Piper said she sometimes stashed treats in her ear to save for later.

contentParagraphs.en: In 2005, she showed researchers that elephants can recognize themselves in a mirror — a sign of self-awareness seen in only a few other species. During the experiment, Happy faced her reflection and repeatedly used her trunk to touch an "X" painted above her eye, a mark she could see only in the mirror.
contentParagraphs.zh: In 2005, she showed researchers that elephants can recognize themselves in a mirror — a sign of self-awareness seen in only a few other species. During the experiment, Happy faced her reflection and repeatedly used her trunk to touch an "X" painted above her eye, a mark she could see only in the mirror.

contentParagraphs.en: She was paired with other elephants until her last partner died in 2006. Happy then lived separately from Patty and a third elephant out of concern that they wouldn't get along, though Lovett said the animals could see, smell and touch each other over a divider. The third elephant, called Maxine, died in 2018.
contentParagraphs.zh: She was paired with other elephants until her last partner died in 2006. Happy then lived separately from Patty and a third elephant out of concern that they wouldn't get along, though Lovett said the animals could see, smell and touch each other over a divider. The third elephant, called Maxine, died in 2018.

contentParagraphs.en: Zoo officials said the median life expectancy for Asian elephants in U.S. zoos is about 45 years. Their life expectancy in the wild is more difficult to pinpoint.
contentParagraphs.zh: Zoo officials said the median life expectancy for Asian elephants in U.S. zoos is about 45 years. Their life expectancy in the wild is more difficult to pinpoint.

contentParagraphs.en: During Happy's lifetime, zoo elephant exhibits came under increased scrutiny. Some experts said urban animal parks were too small for creatures that roam extensive distances in the wild. Animal rights activists argued that zoo enclosures were no place for big-brained, social pachyderms.
contentParagraphs.zh: During Happy's lifetime, zoo elephant exhibits came under increased scrutiny. Some experts said urban animal parks were too small for creatures that roam extensive distances in the wild. Animal rights activists argued that zoo enclosures were no place for big-brained, social pachyderms.

contentParagraphs.en: Some zoos phased out their exhibits and sent elephants to sanctuaries, though some other zoos remain committed to keeping and breeding the creatures, arguing that they help keep people interested in saving wildlife.
contentParagraphs.zh: Some zoos phased out their exhibits and sent elephants to sanctuaries, though some other zoos remain committed to keeping and breeding the creatures, arguing that they help keep people interested in saving wildlife.

contentParagraphs.en: One activist group, the Nonhuman Rights Project, sued the Bronx Zoo in 2018, seeking to have Happy declared a "person" for legal purposes and moved to a large animal sanctuary. It was the first such case about an elephant, according to the group.
contentParagraphs.zh: One activist group, the Nonhuman Rights Project, sued the Bronx Zoo in 2018, seeking to have Happy declared a "person" for legal purposes and moved to a large animal sanctuary. It was the first such case about an elephant, according to the group.

contentParagraphs.en: Citing a principle that's used to challenge the legality of a person's imprisonment, the activist group said Happy was "an extraordinarily cognitively complex and autonomous nonhuman being" who was unlawfully deprived of her liberty and suffered from being pent up in a exhibit without other elephants.
contentParagraphs.zh: Citing a principle that's used to challenge the legality of a person's imprisonment, the activist group said Happy was "an extraordinarily cognitively complex and autonomous nonhuman being" who was unlawfully deprived of her liberty and suffered from being pent up in a exhibit without other elephants.

contentParagraphs.en: Zoo officials said Happy was assiduously cared for and had space for swimming, foraging and other natural behavior. Uprooting her from her longtime home could harm her, the zoo said.
contentParagraphs.zh: Zoo officials said Happy was assiduously cared for and had space for swimming, foraging and other natural behavior. Uprooting her from her longtime home could harm her, the zoo said.

contentParagraphs.en: New York's top court ultimately rejected the activists' claim, by a 5-2 majority. Colorado's highest court later issued a similar ruling about five elephants in a zoo there.
contentParagraphs.zh: New York's top court ultimately rejected the activists' claim, by a 5-2 majority. Colorado's highest court later issued a similar ruling about five elephants in a zoo there.

contentParagraphs.en: Still, two of the New York high court judges wrote pointed dissents. One called Happy's captivity "inherently unjust and inhumane" and "an affront to a civilized society."
contentParagraphs.zh: Still, two of the New York high court judges wrote pointed dissents. One called Happy's captivity "inherently unjust and inhumane" and "an affront to a civilized society."

contentParagraphs.en: The Nonhuman Rights Project has continued pursuing cases about elephants in various other states.
contentParagraphs.zh: The Nonhuman Rights Project has continued pursuing cases about elephants in various other states.

contentParagraphs.en: Happy spent her final weeks, by her choice, in an off-exhibit barn and yard within her enclosure, Piper said. In a zoo version of hospice care, staffers provided hydration, nutrition and pain management, he said.
contentParagraphs.zh: Happy spent her final weeks, by her choice, in an off-exhibit barn and yard within her enclosure, Piper said. In a zoo version of hospice care, staffers provided hydration, nutrition and pain management, he said.

contentParagraphs.en: Meanwhile, Patty is doing well, the zoo said.
contentParagraphs.zh: Meanwhile, Patty is doing well, the zoo said.

contentParagraphs.en: The Wildlife Conservation Society said in 2006 that once there was only one elephant, the animal might be moved to another zoo if circumstances were right. Piper said the zoo will be "really thoughtful and careful" in contemplating whether to move Patty from her home of 53 years.
contentParagraphs.zh: The Wildlife Conservation Society said in 2006 that once there was only one elephant, the animal might be moved to another zoo if circumstances were right. Piper said the zoo will be "really thoughtful and careful" in contemplating whether to move Patty from her home of 53 years.
